# CDISC Data Standards {#sec-cdisc}

::: {.callout-warning}
## Source material missing at scaffold time

The intended source directory
`~/Dropbox/prj/tch/01-phb228-stat-computing/phb228-2026/textbook/cdisc-lecture/`
is not present. This chapter is a content skeleton based on
general CDISC knowledge; please point me at the actual
lecture materials and I will replace this with the
course-specific content.
:::

## Prerequisites

Answer the following questions to see if you can bypass this
chapter. You can find the answers at the end of the chapter in
@sec-quiz-answers-18.

1. What is CDISC, and what organisation sets its standards?
2. What is the difference between SDTM and ADaM, and in
   which order are they produced during a clinical trial?
3. Why must clinical trial sponsors submit CDISC-compliant
   data to the FDA?

## Learning objectives

By the end of this chapter you should be able to:

- Name the principal CDISC standards (CDASH, SDTM, ADaM,
  Define-XML) and the point in the clinical trial workflow
  at which each applies.
- Read an SDTM domain dataset and identify the mandatory
  variables.
- Derive an ADaM dataset from SDTM input following the
  ADaM Implementation Guide principles (traceability,
  one-proc reporting, analysis-ready structure).
- Locate and consult the CDISC standard documents at
  cdisc.org.
- Identify when an R analysis pipeline must produce
  CDISC-compliant output.

## Orientation

CDISC (the Clinical Data Interchange Standards Consortium)
publishes the data standards that pharmaceutical sponsors
must follow when submitting clinical trial data to the FDA.
For a biostatistician working on regulated trials, CDISC
fluency is non-optional; for academic biostatisticians it
is less frequently required but still appears in
investigator-initiated trials and industry collaborations.

## What CDISC covers

TODO: the standards suite.

| Standard     | Purpose                                       |
| ------------ | --------------------------------------------- |
| CDASH        | Data collection (case report form standards)  |
| SDTM         | Tabulation of source data                     |
| ADaM         | Analysis-ready datasets                       |
| Define-XML   | Metadata and documentation                    |
| SEND         | Non-clinical (animal) study data              |

## The SDTM data model

TODO: domain structure (DM, AE, VS, LB, etc.); vertical vs
horizontal organisation; mandatory variables.

## The ADaM data model

TODO: the three principles (analysis-ready, traceability,
one-proc reporting); ADSL; BDS and OCCDS structures.

## Define-XML

TODO: machine-readable metadata describing every dataset,
variable, code list, and derivation.

## CDISC in R

TODO: Pharmaverse ecosystem (`admiral`, `metatools`,
`metacore`, `xportr`); `haven` for reading SAS transport
files; `zzlongplot` and `zztable1` for CDISC-styled output.

## When you need CDISC and when you do not

TODO: FDA submissions vs academic trials; SNF vs IIS; the
'regulatory defensibility' continuum.

## With AI assistance {.unnumbered}

::: {.callout-tip}
## Prompts to try

1. Paste the variable list of a non-CDISC clinical dataset
   and ask the LLM to map it onto SDTM domains. Does it
   correctly identify DM, AE, VS, LB assignments? Which
   variables does it leave unmapped?
2. Ask the LLM to derive a simple ADSL (Subject-Level
   Analysis Dataset) from a SDTM DM domain. Does the
   result satisfy the three ADaM principles?
3. Paste a Define-XML snippet and ask the LLM to summarise
   what it specifies. Does it understand the
   `ItemGroupDef`, `ItemDef`, `MethodDef` elements?
:::

## Exercises

1. Download a publicly available SDTM example dataset
   (CDISC Pilot Project) and identify the mandatory
   variables in the DM, AE, and VS domains.
2. Using the DM domain from exercise 1, derive an ADSL
   following the three ADaM principles. Document every
   derivation in a comment.
3. Produce a Table 1 from the ADSL using `zztable1` with
   `theme = 'regulatory'`. Compare the output to a
   CDISC-style reference table.

## Quiz answers {#sec-quiz-answers-18}

1. CDISC (Clinical Data Interchange Standards Consortium)
   is a global non-profit that develops standards for
   clinical research data. Its standards cover data
   collection (CDASH), tabulation (SDTM), analysis
   (ADaM), and metadata (Define-XML). Membership
   includes pharmaceutical sponsors, CROs, regulators
   (FDA, PMDA), and academic institutions.
2. SDTM (Study Data Tabulation Model) represents source
   data from a clinical trial in a standardised tabular
   format, organised by domain (Demographics, Adverse
   Events, Laboratory, etc.). ADaM (Analysis Data Model)
   represents analysis-ready datasets derived from SDTM,
   structured so a single SAS or R procedure produces
   the final analytic table. SDTM is produced first;
   ADaM is derived from it.
3. Since December 2016 the FDA has required CDISC-
   compliant SDTM and ADaM data as part of electronic
   submissions for NDAs (New Drug Applications) and BLAs
   (Biologics License Applications). Non-compliant
   submissions are refused and must be resubmitted.
